rationale: The cited paper appears highly relevant to the citing paper’s core theory domain: both study dyadic family purchase decisions and influence processes, and both include postdecision satisfaction constructs. The citing note explicitly says it extends prior family decision work and “adds to Corfman and Lehmann’s cooperative decision framework,” while the cited note provides that framework via preference revision/concession and satisfaction links. Given this conceptual overlap, this is not an obvious incidental mention and should be retained for full relation judgment.
